questions.q: What is the key difference between a queue and pub-sub messaging pattern?
options: Queues are faster but less reliable than pub-sub
Queues deliver messages to one consumer; pub-sub broadcasts to all subscribed consumers
Queues require authentication while pub-sub is always anonymous
Pub-sub messages expire faster than queue messages
questions.explanation: Queues provide point-to-point messaging where each message goes to exactly one consumer. Pub-sub provides broadcast messaging where published messages go to all subscribers. Speed, reliability, and authentication aren't inherent differences; expiration is configurable separately.

questions.q: In Kafka, what is a topic?
options: A category of messages organized by publisher name
A distributed log that messages are appended to in order, partitioned across brokers
A connection endpoint to the Kafka cluster
A configuration file describing message schemas
questions.explanation: Kafka topics are distributed logs where producers append messages and consumers read them sequentially. Topics are partitioned for parallelism and distributed across multiple brokers. Topics aren't user-specific, connection endpoints, or schema files.

options: Each message is delivered exactly one time, no more and no less
Each message is delivered one or more times, but duplicates are possible
Each message might not be delivered at all
Messages are delivered out of order but all eventually arrive
questions.explanation: At-least-once guarantees no message loss but allows duplicates if retries occur. It trades duplicate-handling for durability. Exactly-once is stricter; at-most-once allows loss; order is a separate guarantee (often called ordered delivery).

questions.q: In RabbitMQ, what does message acknowledgment (ack) prevent?
options: Confirming that the broker received the message from the publisher
Preventing a consumer from processing the same message multiple times if it crashes before acknowledging
Ensuring the message is encrypted in transit
Allowing the publisher to receive a delivery receipt
questions.explanation: Consumer acknowledgments tell RabbitMQ the message was successfully processed. Without acks, if a consumer crashes, unacknowledged messages are redelivered to another consumer, preventing message loss. Encryption, publisher receipts, and broker-level confirmations are separate features.

questions.q: What is a dead letter queue (DLQ) used for?
options: Messages from deleted publishers
Messages that failed processing after maximum retry attempts
Temporary storage for messages in transit
A private queue accessible only to administrators
questions.explanation: A DLQ holds messages that exceeded retry limits and couldn't be processed successfully, preventing message loss and enabling debugging. It's separate from publisher deletion, message transit, or access control.

questions.q: In Kafka, what is a consumer group?
options: A set of producers publishing to the same topic
Multiple consumers reading from the same topic where each partition is consumed by only one consumer in the group
A configuration that limits how many messages a consumer can process per second
A backup cluster that mirrors the primary cluster
questions.explanation: Consumer groups enable parallel processing where multiple consumers in the same group collectively consume partitions, each partition going to exactly one group member. This prevents duplicate processing while enabling horizontal scaling. Producers form topic definitions; rate limits and mirroring are separate.

questions.q: What is the advantage of using message brokers over direct HTTP calls between services?
options: HTTP calls are insecure while message brokers are always encrypted
Message brokers decouple services temporally and handle retries, backpressure, and async processing
Message brokers are faster because they use binary protocols
Direct HTTP calls don't scale beyond 100 services
questions.explanation: Message brokers enable loose coupling: services don't need to be online simultaneously, the broker handles retries and queuing, and processing can be async. HTTP is also valid for many use cases but is synchronous and tightly coupled. Binary protocols aren't inherent; HTTP and message brokers both support various protocols.

questions.q: What does 'partitioning' or 'sharding' in Kafka enable?
options: Encrypting messages so different partitions can't read each other's data
Distributing a topic across multiple broker nodes for parallelism and fault tolerance
Replicating messages to backup servers
Creating separate topics for different message types
questions.explanation: Partitioning splits a topic across brokers, allowing multiple consumers to process messages in parallel and improving throughput and fault tolerance. Partitions aren't separate topics; they're divisions within a topic. Encryption and replication are separate features.

questions.q: In RabbitMQ, what is an exchange?
options: A protocol for communicating with other message brokers
A routing component that receives messages from producers and delivers them to queues based on binding rules
A cache layer that stores frequently accessed messages
An API endpoint for consuming messages
questions.explanation: Exchanges route messages from publishers to one or more queues using rules (direct, topic, fanout, headers). Producers send to exchanges, not directly to queues. Exchanges aren't broker protocols, caches, or endpoints.

questions.q: What is the difference between fanout and topic exchange types in RabbitMQ?
options: Fanout is faster; topic is more flexible
Fanout delivers to all bound queues; topic delivers based on routing key patterns matching queue binding keys
Fanout requires authentication; topic is always anonymous
Topic is deprecated and should not be used
questions.explanation: Fanout broadcasts to all queues regardless of routing keys (one-to-many broadcast). Topic exchange routes based on wildcard patterns in routing keys (one-to-many selective). Speed, authentication, and deprecation aren't distinguishing factors.
